zotero.abstractNote: Large-scale population-based imaging studies provide a unique opportunity to study the association of organ-specific morphology with health and disease. However, the manual analysis of such large datasets is infeasible. We developed a fully automated pipeline for the segmentation of six abdominal organs (liver, spleen, right and left kidney, pancreas, and gallbladder) in abdominal magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) data. The pipeline is based on a deep learning approach using a nnU-Net architecture. We trained and validated the model on 400 manually annotated datasets from the UK Biobank and the German National Cohort (NAKO). We then applied the pipeline to 20,000 participants of the UK Biobank and NAKO. Furthermore, we developed a quality control (QC) classifier to automatically identify segmentation failures. The segmentation pipeline achieved high Dice similarity coefficients (DSC) for all organs (mean DSC 0.95 for liver, 0.94 for spleen, 0.94 for kidneys, 0.84 for pancreas, and 0.82 for gallbladder). The QC classifier achieved an area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC) of 0.98 for the detection of segmentation failures. Our pipeline enables the large-scale analysis of abdominal organ morphology in population-based imaging studies.
